coredata.dc:title: Assembling carbon dots on vertically aligned acetate fibers as ideal salt-rejecting evaporators for solar water purification
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Haoyang
Xue, Chaorui
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: A low-cost and anti-salt-fouling system to perform solar interfacial water purification in long-term stability is considered to be a promising ways to solve freshwater scarcity crisis globally. However, the present self-regenerating solar evaporators are still confronted with challenges in terms of the relatively low evaporation rate, unsatisfactory solar-to-vapor efficiency, intricate or costly preparation steps. Herein, we present a novel and controllable system established by assembling carbon dots (CDs) on vertically aligned acetate fibers (VAAFs) with very low thermal conductivity and excellent water transport capacity. As-prepared VAAFs@CDs-T can spontaneously reject salt accumulation through rapid fluid convection within the evaporating interfaces and simultaneously sustain efficient heat localization. Thus, it not only possesses superior salt-rejection capability in long-term operation, but also enables considerably high water evaporation rate at 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 with 93.9% solar-to-steam efficiency under one sun even treating with 20 wt% salt solution and industrial wastewater. Total materials cost of this system is merely $15.01 m−2, outperforming most previous solar evaporators in the cost-effectiveness. Moreover, CDs can be replaced with other zero-dimensional materials and hereby achieving further enhanced evaporation efficiency is hopeful for this system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar interfacial water purification
Carbon dots
Seawater desalination
Self-regenerating solar evaporator
Assembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of stability, cost and self-desalting mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi
Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi , China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China

Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province , China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi , China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province ( 201903D421082 , 201803D421091 ), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China; Graduate Education Innovation Program of Shanxi Province ( 2019SY428 ), China.

This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082, 201803D421091), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China; Graduate Education Innovation Program of Shanxi Province (2019SY428), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration for the preparation process of VAAFs@CDs-T; (B) TEM image of CDs, the inset showing the HRTEM image of a nanoparticle; (C and D) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs aggregated on VAAFs; (E and F) TEM and HRTEM images of heat-treated CDs on VAAFs; (G) TGA patterns of pure VAAFs and VAAFs@CDs, the inset showing the photo of heat-treated VAAFs.
SEM images of VAAFs (A, B), VAAFs@CDs (C–E, Top view; F–H, Lateral view) and VAAFs@CDs-T (I, J, Top view; K–L, Lateral view).

(A) UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of VAAFs, VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T; (B) Comparisons of thermal conductivities for different photothermal materials; (C) Time course of the temperature for the surface of VAAFs, VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T under one sun; (D) Mass change as a function of time for VAAFs, VAAFs-T, VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T under one sun; (E) Comparisons of the evaporation performances of VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T before and after soaking in water for 2 h; (F) PL comparisons of the soaked solution of VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T, the inset showing the solution photos under 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) Mass change as a function of time under various salinities of NaCl solution; (B) Comparisons of evaporation rates under various salinities; (C) Comparisons of evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency of VAAFs@CDs-T under one sun with other evaporation system reported in literatures (R1–R14) [9,27,29,30,47–56]; (D) Effects of solar irradiation intensities on the evaporation performances of VAAFs@CDs-T in 20 wt% NaCl solution; (E, F) Photos for salt-free surface of VAAFs@CDs-T and salt crystal formation surface of carbonized basswood under continuous evaporation in 20 wt% NaCl solution, respectively; (G) Water purity test using a multimeter under the given distance between electrodes.

(A) The durability of VAAFs@CDs-T worked in the 20 wt% NaCl solution; (B) Repeated useperformance of VAAFs@CDs-T in the 20 wt% NaCl solution; (C) Dependence of the wicking rate on the tortuosity of VAFFs at the given porosity; (D) Schematic illustration of the anti-salt mechanism of VAAFs@CDs-T; (E, F) Water transport status at different time point for VAAFs and basswood.
Schematic illustrations of electronic level structures of the separated CDs (A) and their assemblies (B).

head.ce:title: Assembling carbon dots on vertically aligned acetate fibers as ideal salt-rejecting evaporators for solar water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A low-cost and anti-salt-fouling system to perform solar interfacial water purification in long-term stability is considered to be a promising ways to solve freshwater scarcity crisis globally. However, the present self-regenerating solar evaporators are still confronted with challenges in terms of the relatively low evaporation rate, unsatisfactory solar-to-vapor efficiency, intricate or costly preparation steps. Herein, we present a novel and controllable system established by assembling carbon dots (CDs) on vertically aligned acetate fibers (VAAFs) with very low thermal conductivity and excellent water transport capacity. As-prepared VAAFs@CDs-T can spontaneously reject salt accumulation through rapid fluid convection within the evaporating interfaces and simultaneously sustain efficient heat localization. Thus, it not only possesses superior salt-rejection capability in long-term operation, but also enables considerably high water evaporation rate at 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 with 93.9% solar-to-steam efficiency under one sun even treating with 20 wt% salt solution and industrial wastewater. Total materials cost of this system is merely $15.01 m−2, outperforming most previous solar evaporators in the cost-effectiveness. Moreover, CDs can be replaced with other zero-dimensional materials and hereby achieving further enhanced evaporation efficiency is hopeful for this system.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-driven water purification from seawater or wastewater has emerged as one of the most economic, sustainable and promising solutions to meet the increasing freshwater demand [1,2]. This mainlythanks to the advent of solar interfacial water evaporation (SIWE) technique with ideal solar-to-steam conversion efficiencies [2–4]. The most prominent feature of SIWE is the position of the light-to-heat conversion agent, which is at the interfaces between liquid water and the above air [5]. Such special configuration not only localizes solar heat at the water-air interface avoiding wastefully heating underlying bulk water, but also offers remarkably larger surface area for rapid steam release [2,5–7]. Obviously, the photothermal material in SIWE system is a key component influencing final conversion efficiency since it determines sunlight capture and following photothermal conversion [2,5,8].

Comparing with semiconductor and plasmonic materials, carbon-based nanomaterials show higher potential for large-scale applications owing to their excellent sunlight harvesting, low cost, and environmental friendliness [6,8–13]. Bulk carbon black, active carbon and graphite are able to absorb broad-band light absorption visible light due to the π-band's optical transitions [6,14,15], but their absorption properties are limited by a moderate reflection of 5–10% at air-dielectric interface. Although this limitation can be overcome through large amount of microcavities formed by assembling carbon nanostructures like graphene, carbon nanotube, these crystalline carbons with their high inherent thermal conductivity enabling fast heat dissipation in the whole material are actually unfavorable for heat localization [6,8,16–18]. Accordingly, these black materials showed relatively low water evaporation rate as well as photothermal conversion efficiency. By contrast, carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles consisting of carbon cores that can be surrounded by shells containing numerous hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxylic acids, epoxies, alcohols, carbonyls and amines [19–22], thus own an ideal geometry in terms of heat preservation [8,23]. The nature of these groups arises from the precursors and the preparing conditions employed in the CD synthesis [20–22]. In particular, these functional groups control the interaction of the CDs with the surrounding environment in terms of solvation, reactivity, binding and others [19,20,22]. For instance, surface hydrophilic groups of the CDs can form various hydrogen bonds with water molecules [18], resulting in weakly hydrogen-bonded intermediate water that influences the overall energy demand of vapor generation [4,24]. Thereby, their assemblies not only are endowed with a low thermal conductivity and a unique ability to reduce vaporization enthalpy by means of water molecule adsorption effect, but also allow heat to be confined and channeled to a specific location when they are incorporated with an appropriate insulating material [25]. Unfortunately, there are still few researches on the use of CDs to design SIWE system so far.

Long-term sustainable and high-efficiency evaporation using SIWE technique is desired for seawater desalination or wastewater purification [5,26,27]. However, the accumulation of salt or contaminant at the heating interface can severely deteriorate steam generation [5,28]. Therefore, reliable strategies are needed to develop for anti-salt fouling in solar desalination process. Typically passive ways to reject salt, i.e. by simple rinsing/washing, natural dissolution and even site-specific salt precipitation, commonly spend manpower and physical resources and inevitably make solar absorber property degenerate for long-term operation [5]. Recently, new promising solutions to address salt clogging issue have been explored by designing ingenious structures. For instance, Janus structure that is made from the self-healing hydrophobic layer on the top surface and the hydrophilic layer at the bottom has shown an effective ability to avoid salt formation [29]. Nevertheless, this hydrophobic design has relatively low efficiency (<60%) for solar-to-steam conversion due to imperfect heat management [26,29]. Alternatively, Kuang et al. reported a self-regenerating solar evaporator by directly drilling millimeter-sized channels within surface carbonized basswood [30]. The designed artificial channel-arrays together with the microchannels of wood enable more rapid water convection and solute exchange, avoiding hypersaline fluid production during intensive evaporation. Although this solar evaporator can spontaneously reject salt for long-term continuous operation, it has only about 1.04 kg m−2 h−1 of evaporation rate with ≈75% solar-to-vapor efficiency under 1 sun irradiation (1 kW m−2). Moreover, several salt-rejecting SIWE systems were constructed by employing natural plant fibers as the support of photothermal agents because of their good thermal insulator properties [31–34]. For instance, the solar evaporator fabricated using candle soot-modified natural linen fibers as a solar receiver achieved an evaporation rate of 1.447 kg m−2 h−1 in 20 wt% saline water under 1 sun irradiation [33]. The kapok fiber-supported polypyrroles exhibited an evaporation rate of 1.2792 kg m−2 h−1 in 3.5 wt% saline water under 1 sun irradiation [31]. Plasmonic functionalized cotton nanocomposite fibers showed an evaporation rate of 1.4 kg m−2 h−1 in artificial seawater under 1 sun irradiation [32]. It is clearly found that all of these evaporators still possess low evaporation rates, thus limiting their practical applications in seawater desalination.

Based on self-regeneration mechanism of anti-salt-fouling function, herein we designed a new solar interfacial evaporator by assembling CDs on vertically aligned acetate fibers (VAAFs), which are a green, environment-friendly and reproducible material that has good thermal insulation property and superior adsorption capacity [35]. CDs were used from our reported method that had the advantages of energy conservation, high productivity and easy realization of large-scale production [36,37]. A unique feature of these CDs is easy to assemble by gradually removing the solvent. By this means, CDs can evenly arrange on VAAFs and form photothermal agents. We found that our developed evaporator not only possesses superior salt-rejection capability and cycling stability, but also exhibits significantly high evaporation rate (2.6 kg m−2 h−1) and solar-to-vapor efficiency (93.9%). Accordingly, our findings demonstrated an available strategy for designing salt-rejecting system with zero dimensional nanostructured materials to perform ideal solar desalination.

VAAFs were purchased from cigarettes factory (Huangguoshu Tobacco Group Co. LTD, Guiyang, China) without further treatment. CDs were prepared by our method reported previously [36]. As-prepared CDs’ powder was dispersed in absolute ethyl alcohol by ultrasonic agitation for 15 min in room temperature to obtain the solution of 20 g L−1 concentration. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, VAAFs was firstly saturated with water. It should be pointed out that this step is crucial for blocking the downward diffusion of CD’s ethanol solution. Secondly, taking 1 mL of CD’s ethanol solution was uniformly dripped on the top surface of VAAFs. Thirdly, the sample was put into a drying oven and heated at 60 °C for removing water to obtain VAAFs@CDs. Finally, the top surface with loading CDs was heated at 240 °C for 8 s to obtain VAAFs@CDs-T.

VAAFs derived from fabrication technique of cigarette filters were required to soak firstly in water before dripping ethanol solution of CDs into their top surface as shown in Fig. 1A. The adsorbedwaterin VAAFs can retard the downward diffusion of CDs due to the surface tension difference between water and ethanol, thus confining CDs on the surface layer of VAAFs. After drying, the system assembling CDs on VAAFs (VAAFs@CDs) was produced. CDs are relatively stable on VAAFs due to their strong adsorption, but they can be still detached from the system gradually during long-term operation in water. Therefore, the top surface assembled CDs was further treated by heating at 240 °C for 8 s to make acetate fibers melt, leading to a highly stable system named as VAAFs@CDs-T. As shown in Fig. S1, the lower temperature can’t make fiber surface oxidation fully and influence light harvesting of the surface, while the higher temperature causes fiber surface carbonization facilely as well as hydrophobic property at the surface and thus deteriorates water transport.

By transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization, the used CDs are well-dispersed in ethanol solution as well as having uniform sizes with several nanometers (Fig. 1B). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image illustrates the crystal structure feature of CDs with well-resolved uniform lattice fringes (shown in the inset of Fig. 1B). The observed interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm can be assigned to the (100) graphite plane. Fig. 1C shows the TEM image of as-prepared VAAFs@CDs. It can be observed to aggregateCDs together. Further HRTEM image in Fig. 1D reveals that CDs are adjacently located and possess the identical interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm. Therefore, the dispersive CDs have been assembled on VAAFs after processing via our presented method mentioned above. Fig. 1E and F display the TEM images of as-prepared VAAFs@CDs-T with different magnifications. Clearly, it is difficult to observe the crystal structure feature of CDs due to the organic coating on their surface. This should result from the melt of acetate fibers under heating. Fig. 1G provides the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) patterns of VAAFs and VAAFs@CDs. Both samples exhibit an initial weight loss from 100 to 180 °C due to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water. The second weight loss starts from 180 to 320 °C for pure VAAFs while 180 to 280 °C for VAAFs@CDs could be originated from the removal of oxygen-contained functional groups from their surface through oxidation reaction [38]. In this temperature range, acetate fibers are able to melt and thereby the morphology of VAAFs will be easily destroyed on account of volume shrinkage as shown in the inset of Fig. 1G. For VAAFs@CDs, CDs markedly facilitate the oxidation and decomposition of VAAFs at lower temperature by introducing more oxygen-related groups. As a result, the assembly of CDs on the surface of VAAFs effectively keeps the original morphology unchanged as illustrated in Fig. 1A.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of VAAFs reveal that the size of fibers is uniform, with a diameter of around 20 μm (Fig. 2 A). Further magnification shows that the surface of fibers with a pitaya stem-like shape is smooth (Fig. 2B). After assembling CDs on their surface, SEM images of VAAFs@CDs depict that each of fibers has been grafted with large amounts of CDs assemblies from the top view (Fig. 2C–E) while the grooves on each surface of fibers are fully covered by CDs assemblies from the lateral view (Fig. 2F–H). This demonstrates that CDs have been assembled on the surface of VAAFs. After heating treatment of VAAFs@CDs, the resultant VAAFs@CDs-T shows apparently melted appearance at tail ends of fibers on the basis of the top view of SEM images (Fig. 2I and J). Simultaneously, this case is also certified by the cross section view of its SEM images (Fig. 2K and L), which present smooth surface again and almost disappearance of the surface groove. One can safely deduce that CDs are embedded into fibers via heating treatment. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns showed no obvious differences due to ultrafine sizes of CDs (Fig. S2). The chemical composition of different samples was further analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. From Fig. S3, FTIR spectrum of VAAFs displays the typical stretching peaks belonging to cellulose acetate; and FTIR spectra of VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T contain the combination of characteristic peaks of both CDs and VAAFs, thereby confirming the presence of CDs on VAAFs.

For separated CDs, they usually possess unique characteristics akin to semiconductor quantum dots (Scheme 1 A), but their electronic energy levels depend on their surface states [25]. Thus the combination of CDs with themselves and other substances will induce a series of sublevels between conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) as depicted in Scheme 1B, losing their luminescent properties (Fig. S4). Consequently, the aggregation/assemblies of CDs not only broaden their light absorption ranges, but also promote nonradiative recombination that always releases heat energy [25]. Fig. 3 A displays UV–Vis–near-infrared (UV–Vis–NIR) absorption of VAAFs, VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T. It can be clearly observed that the naked VAAFs have almost no solar absorption over a wide wavelength range from 400 to 1200 nm. After assembling CDs on them, VAAFs@CDs present a similar absorption features with solar irradiation spectrum as well as strong absorption at the range from 400 to 1200 nm. Therefore, the roles of CDs assemblies are crucial in solar harvesting for VAAFs@CDs system. Interestingly, we also found that the solar absorption of VAAFs@CDs-T is improved significantly and the corresponding intensity is markedly higher than that of VAAFs@CDs. This demonstrates that heating treatment not only endows high stability of VAAFs@CDs-T, but also enhances its solar-harvesting ability. Moreover, as-prepared samples have considerably low thermal conductivities varying from 0.0565 to 0.0641 W m−1 K−1, which are much smaller than those of woods/carbonized woods and other reported photothermal materials [30,39,40] (Fig. 3B).

The solar water evaporation performance of VAAFs, VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T was evaluated under one sun. Upon irradiation, the temperature of their surface rapidly increases due to the heat localization induced by light-to-heat conversion of photothermal materials, as recorded by the thermal couple (Fig. 3C) and IR camera (Fig. S5). Comparing to VAAFs, the surface temperature of VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T increases more sharply at the initial transient period and is much higher at the steady period than the bulk water temperature (Fig. S6), indicating that the CDs assemblies promote local heat generation. Additionally, VAAFs@CDs-T exhibits larger temperature increment at the initial stage and slightly lower surface temperature at the steady stage than VAAFs@CDs. This case could be attributed to better thermal management of VAAFs@CDs-T than VAAFs@CDs. As shown in Fig. 3D, the evaporation rates of water with VAAFs, VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T were measured by recording the weight loss as a function of time under solar illumination of 1 kW m−2. For comparison, we also conducted the water evaporation rates of VAAFs-T, which was obtained by heating the surface of pure VAAFs with similar processing of VAAFs@CDs-T, under the same conditions. The calculated results verify that the water evaporation rates for VAAFs, VAAFs-T, VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T are ca. 1.4 ± 0.05, 0.9 ± 0.03, 2.3 ± 0.07 and 2.6 ± 0.08 kg m−2 h−1, respectively. Obviously, the evaporation rate of VAAFs@CDs-T is 1.9 and 2.9 times higher than the naked VAAFs and VAAFs-T. By contrast, VAAFs-T exhibits the lowest evaporation rate of water thanks to losing vertically aligned configurations and original hydrophilic natures of VAAFs (See the inset of Fig. 1G).

We also estimated the water evaporation rate of surface carbonized basswood reported in literature employing our testing setup and conditions. From Fig. S7, the obtained evaporation rate of surface carbonized basswood is in keepingwith the reported value [30], whereas lower than that of VAAFs. Despite that surface carbonized basswood possesses better sunlight harvesting than VAAFs [30], its water evaporation performance is still inferior to VAAFs. This implies that the performance of evaporator is not completely determined by its absorption property. For instance, water supply is another key factor to influence evaporation [5]. Thereby, the height of samples above the water was investigated as well. As shown in Fig. S8, the height of 1.5 cm above the water delivered the highest water evaporation rate of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1. The added height increases the transport distance of water, leading to short supply of water during solar steam generation [5]. On the contrary, the reduced height enables excessive water supply for water evaporation, inducing heat loss to water. Moreover, we explored the effects of the loading amount of CDs on the evaporation performance of VAAFs@CDs-T, and found that the optimal amount is around 130 g m−2 (Fig. S9).

The energy efficiency (η) for solar-steam conversion can be estimated by [25]
(1)
η =
m ̇
H e
C
opt
q 0
where ṁ, C opt, q 0 are evaporation rate, optical concentration, solar irradiation power of 1 sun, respectively. He represents evaporation enthalpy of the water in photothermal materials and can be obtained by the following equation [24],
(2)
H e
m e
=
H 0
m 0
H 0 and m 0 are evaporation enthalpy and evaporation rate of bulk water without illumination, respectively; me refers to the evaporation rate of evaporator (VAAFs@CDs-T) at the same condition. By experimental testing (Table S1), the obtained He of the water in VAAFs@CDs-T was 1301 kJ kg−1. As a result, the conversion efficiency of solar steam generation for VAAFs@CDs-T reached up to 93.9% (Fig. 4 C). Apart from the hydration ability of reducing He
[4,7], superior heat localization ability and advanced water management from VAAFs@CDs-T could be also contributed to its high evaporation performance.

Fig. 3E compares the stabilities of VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T. With 2 h of soaking in water, VAAFs@CDs gets visibly worse while VAAFs@CDs-T keeps unchanged in evaporation performance. Consequently, VAAFs@CDs is difficult to evaporate stably for a long time due to the exfoliation of CDs. To confirm it, we further collected the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the soaked solution produced from VAAFs@CDs and VAAFs@CDs-T. The solution from VAAFs@CDs exhibits strong fluorescence emission, whereas that of VAAFs@CDs-T did not emit any obvious fluorescence (Fig. 3F). This demonstrates that the heating treatment reinforces the coupling of CDs with VAAFs, avoiding the exfoliation of CDs from VAAFs.

The solar evaporation performance of VAAFs@CDs-T was carefully evaluated in a series of NaCl solutions with different concentrations varying from 0 to 25 wt% (Fig. 4A and B). The salinity of brine does not influence the evaporation rate of VAAFs@CDs-T until it reaches to 25 wt%, which is close to the saturation concentration of salt in water. VAAFs@CDs-T exhibits highly stable water evaporation performance of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 in contrast with the conventional solar evaporators in NaCl brine. Although there are also some published works achieving high water evaporation rate and solar-to-steam efficiency [41–46], we wish to point out that many of them suffer from salt accumulation on the heating surface without self-regeneration function that can make solar evaporator continuously work with no salt production on the heating surface [5,30]. As for black carbon nanostructures as photothermal materials, the evaporation rate and efficiency of VAAFs@CDs-T in brine is remarkably higher than that of the salt-rejecting evaporators reported in literatures [9,27,29,30,47–56] (Fig. 4C and Table S2). Furthermore, the effects of solar irradiation intensities on the solar evaporation performances of the VAAFs@CDs-T evaporator in 20 wt% NaCl solution were explored and shown in Fig. 4D. Under 5 sun irradiation, VAAFs@CDs-T increase evaporation rate more than triple and still no salt accumulation was observed on its surface, demonstrating its superior adaptability to different working conditions.

The solar desalination performance of VAAFs@CDs-T was also investigated under long-term operation. After 6 h continuous measuring in 20 wt% NaCl solution under 1 sun, we did not observe any salt deposition on the surface of VAAFs@CDs-T, suggesting that it has an intrinsic salt-rejecting ability (Fig. 4E). Surface-carbonized basswood was used for control experiment. Fig. 4F clearly illustrates that a layer of precipitated salt is accumulated on its surface after continuous operation in the 20 wt% NaCl solution for one hour under 1 sun irradiation. The accumulated salt at the surface will significantly weaken the solar absorption of photothermal materials underneath due to solar reflectance increase, causing the deterioration of the evaporator rate in desalination (Fig. S10). The purified water quality was estimated by its resistance [3]. The ohmic values of different water samples were obtained by a multimeter without changing distance between electrodes (Fig. 4G). The 20 wt% NaCl solution, purified water, domestic water (Taiyuan, China), and DI Water in lab displays resistance values of 1.775, 92.2, 56.7 and 99.7 kΩ, respectively, indicating that the purified water quality is very close to DI water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of stability, cost and self-desalting mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluating practical applicability of VAAFs@CDs-T, its long-term mechanical stability in underwater working condition was performed in 20 wt% NaCl solution for 10 days under natural sunlight via a home-made setup (Fig. S11). We did not observe salt crystal formation and accumulation on the surface of VAAFs@CDs-T (Fig. S12) and any solar evaporation performance decay (Fig. 5 A). In addition, we verified the reused reliability of VAAFs@CDs-T through repeating the drying-utilizing process. A stable evaporation rate was also achieved after recycling 16 times in 20 wt% NaCl solution with one sun illumination (Fig. 5B). All of above results confirm the excellent mechanical stability and structural durability of VAAFs@CDs-T. Accordingly, VAAFs@CDs-T can be continuously running in long term under high salinity seawater without incurring evaporation performance decay and repeated use for cost reduction. What’s more, the materials cost of VAAFs@CDs-T is merely 15.01 $ m−2 (Tables S3 and S4), which also outperforms previously reported evaporation systems in terms of cost-effectiveness (Table S5). Following the calculated method reported by Chen et al. [14], we also estimated life cycle cost of sea water desalination using our presented solar evaporator, which is about $2.17 m−3 of clean water generated.

The intrinsic anti-salt-fouling performance of VAAFs@CDs-T could originate from fluid convection efficiency between solar evaporator and water reservoir, which enables high concentration brine at the evaporation interfaces to be diluted before the precipitation of salt crystals occurs [5]. That is, VAAFs@CDs-T possesses a wick structure that can provide unblocked pathways for rapid water transport and salt exchange. According to Darcy’s law, the flow rate through VAFFs can be expressed as [57]:
(3)
Q =
∅
d
2
32 μ τ
8 σ cos θ
dL
- ρ g
where Q is the flow rate (kg m−2 h−1), μ is the water viscosity, τ is the tortuosity of VAAFs, d is the diameter of VAAFs, L is the length of VAAFs, Φ is the porosity, σ is the air-water surface tension (0.072 N m−1), θ is the contact angle, ρ is the water density, and g is the gravity acceleration. Considering θ as zero due to good hydrophilcity of VAAFs, the dependence of Q on the τ is plotted at the given Φ in Fig. 5C. The calculated result proved that the low tortuosity is beneficial to water convection enhancement, agreeing well with the reported experimental measurements [58].

As illustrated in Fig. 5D, each of acetate fibers should be responsible for rapid water supply rather than aligned micro-channels and vertically oriented pores as reported previously [30,58]. Along with grooves on the surface of fibers, water from reservoir is transported sufficiently and continuously to evaporation sites by virtue of capillary effect. Meanwhile, three types of water, i.e. free water, intermediate water, and bound water, are formed by different intermolecular hydrogen bonding [24]. The intermediate water is mainly in charge of vapor phase transformation via less energy consumption while the sufficient free water acts as diluent in localized space [24]. The spaces among fibers offer sufficiently spatial conditions for advection and diffusion induced by salt concentration gradient, thus resulting in no salt crystallization during intensive water evaporation. Unlike natural water evaporation [59], the reduction in water vapor pressure at the water surface owing to an increase of the water salinity could have little effect on the evaporation rate of VAAFs@CDs-T during the SIWE process.

To clarify the properties of directional water transport, Fig. 5E depicts the transport behavior of ink (used for visual contrast) in the VAAFs. Clearly, the ink water is rapidly transported to the top of VAAFs (2.5 cm in length) in a very short period of time (≈7 s) by capillary force. On the contrary, the ink water transport in the basswood is very slow, showing a time of 210 s to reach the top of basswood having same length with VAAFs (Fig. 5F). As demonstrated previously [5,58], the worse water transport of basswood is due to its high tortuosity induced the random channels. Also this leads to poor salt-rejection property of surface carbonized basswood as solar evaporator. The CDs’ assembly layer on the top surface of VAAFs did not influence their wettability (Video 1). VAAFs@CDs-T as solar evaporator still hold fast water transport feature originated from the superior wick structure of VAAFs, thereby resulting in highly efficient fluid convection in channels among fibers for rejecting salt formation.

Moreover, the effects of organic contaminants including of nitrophenol (4-NP), o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB) were examined (Fig. S13). We did not observed any related contaminants in the collected water based on the measured results of UV–Vis absorption spectra for the condensed water. This case mainly owes superior adsorption performance of VAAFs, thereby effectively restricting organic contaminants access to vapor. Therefore, our presented VAAFs@CDs-T system is highly reliable for solar water evaporation to realize seawater desalination, water purification and regeneration to solve water crisis globally. More importantly, CDs on the surface of VAAFs can be also replaced with other zero-dimensional materials to make new evaporators. To demonstrate it, we assembled our previously prepared nanocomposite of CDs-stabilized paramelaconite (CDs@Cu4O3) [60] on the surface of VAAFs by the same method. From Fig. S14, it displayed an evaporation rate of ca. 2.3 kg m−2 h−1 in brine.

We demonstrated a novel water evaporation system assembling zero-dimensional CDs on VAAFs with good thermal insulation property for anti-salt-fouling solar interfacial desalination. The presented VAAFs@CDs-T can sustain excellent heat localization and spontaneously reject salt crystallization through highly efficient fluid convection within the evaporating interfaces. Accordingly, it not only has superior salt-rejection capability and cycling stability, but also enables considerably large water evaporation rate at 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 with 93.9% solar-to-steam efficiency under one sun irradiation even treating with high concentration seawater and industrial wastewater. The combination of the superior performances and the low-cost and environmental friendliness of materials could endow this system great potential as a salt-rejecting and long-term stable solar evaporator for seawater desalination, wastewater purification and metal extractions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082, 201803D421091), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China; Graduate Education Innovation Program of Shanxi Province (2019SY428), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.129822.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1
